root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

function spaces

Table of contents

  1. Spaces of integrable functions
  2. Spaces of continuous functions
  3. Continuous functions and integration
  4. Interlude: locally convex spaces
  5. Spaces of differentiable functions
  6. References

Spaces of integrable functions

The $L^p$-spaces of real analysis are some of the most important function spaces in mathematics, and one spends a significant amount of time in their first course on measure and integration studying their properties. My discussion will therefore be quite brief.

First, their definition:

Definition. Fix a measure space $(X,\mfM,\mu)$.

  • For each positive real number $p$ and each measurable function $f: X \to \bbc$, we define \begin{equation}\notag \norm{f}_p = \left( \int_X |f|^p \ \d \mu \right)^{1/p}. \end{equation}

  • For $p= \infty$, we define \begin{equation}\label{ess-eqn} \norm{f}_\infty = \inf \{ c\geq 0 : \mu(\{ x: |f(x)| > c \}) = 0 \}. \end{equation}

Then, for each fixed $p$ with $0 < p \leq \infty$, we declare two functions $f_1$ and $f_2$ in the set

\begin{equation}\notag \{f : \text{$f:X \to \bbc$ measurable and $\norm{f}_p<\infty$}\} \end{equation}

to be equivalent if $f_1=f_2$ almost everywhere, and we define $L^p(X,\mfM,\mu)$, called an $L^p$-space, to be the set of equivalence classes.

Some remarks regarding the $L^p$-spaces:

  • One often writes $L^p(X)$, $L^p(\mu)$, or even $L^p$ in place of $L^p(X,\mfM,\mu)$ if the data defining the $L^p$-space is understood or clear from context.

  • If $\mu$ is the counting measure and $\mfM$ is the power set, it is traditional to write $l^p(X)$ in place of $L^p(X)$. The notation $l^p$ is often reserved for $l^p(\bbn)$.

  • Though technically elements in them are equivalence classes of functions, one often still treats the $L^p$-spaces as if they were sets of functions.

  • The number \eqref{ess-eqn} is sometimes called the essential supremum, and is written \begin{equation}\notag \norm{f}_\infty = \essSup_{x\in X}\ {|f(x)|}. \end{equation}

Each of the $L^p$-spaces (for $1\leq p \leq \infty$) is a normed $\bbc$-vector space with respect to pointwise addition and scaling, and with $\norm{\cdot}_p$ furnishing the norm. Moreover:

Theorem. Let $(X,\mfM,\mu)$ be a measure space, and $p$ a number with $1\leq p \leq \infty$.

  1. For each $p$ with $1\leq p \leq \infty$, the vector space $L^p(X)$ is a Banach space.

  2. The space $L^2(X)$ is a Hilbert space, with inner product given by \begin{equation}\notag \ang{f,g} = \int_X f(x) \overline{g(x)} \ \d \mu(x). \end{equation}

If $X$ is any set whatsoever, notice that $l^\infty(X)$ is the Banach space of all bounded functions $f:X \to \bbc$. Indeed, in this case, the norm $\norm{\cdot}_\infty$ is simply the familiar uniform norm:

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{f}_\infty = \sup_{x\in X}|f(x)|. \end{equation}

There are a few important subspaces of the $L^p$-spaces that are dense; the first is given in:

Theorem. Let $(X,\mfM,\mu)$ be a measure space and let $p$ be a number with $1 \leq p \leq \infty$.

  • If $p$ is finite, then the set of all simple functions of the form \begin{equation}\notag f = \sum_{k=1}^n a_k \chi_{A_k}, \quad a_k \in \bbc, \end{equation} are dense in $L^p(X)$, provided that $\mu(A_k) < \infty$ for each $k$.

  • If $p=\infty$, then the set of all simple functions is dense in $L^\infty(X)$.

Besides being Banach spaces, the $L^\infty$-spaces have compatible multiplicative and involutive structures:

Theorem. Let $(X,\mfM,\mu)$ be a measure space and let $f,g\in L^\infty(X)$.

  • Define the product of $f$ and $g$, denoted $fg$, to be their pointwise product: \begin{equation}\notag (fg)(x) = f(x) g(x), \quad \forall x\in X. \end{equation}
  • Define the involution $f\mapsto f^\ast$ on $L^\infty(X)$ to be given by pointwise complex conjugation: \begin{equation}\notag f^\ast(x) = \overline{f(x)}, \quad \forall x\in X. \end{equation}

With respect to these operations, the pointwise vector space operations, and the norm $\norm{\cdot}_\infty$, the space $L^\infty(X)$ is a commutative, unital $\upC^\ast$-algebra.

In particular, the space $l^\infty(X)$ of all bounded complex-valued functions on a set $X$ is a unital $\upC^\ast$-algebra.

Definition. Fix an integer $n\geq 1$ and let $U\subseteq \bbr^n$ be a nonempty open subset. A Lebesgue measurable function $f: U \to \bbc$ is called locally integrable if

\begin{equation}\notag \int_K |f(x)| \ \d^n x <\infty \end{equation}

for every compact subset $K\subseteq U$. Here, $\d^n x$ is the standard Lebesgue measure on $\bbr^n$. If we declare two locally integrable functions to be equivalent if they agree almost everywhere, then we define the $L^1_{\text{loc}}(U)$ to be the collection of all equivalence classes of locally integrable functions.

Note that $L^1_{\text{loc}}(U)$ is a $\bbc$-vector space. We will discuss its topological structure later after having introduced locally convex spaces and Fréchet spaces.

Spaces of continuous functions

Having studied spaces of integrable functions on measure spaces, we now look at spaces of continuous functions on topological spaces. By the end of this section, we will have five such spaces:

\begin{equation}\label{cont-eqn} C(X), \quad C(X;A), \quad C_b(X), \quad C_c(X), \quad C_0(X). \end{equation}

To help organize our thoughts and the various algebraic and topological structures on these spaces, it will be helpful to consider first the set $\bbc^X$ of all functions $f:X \to \bbc$ on a set $X$, not assumed at the outset to have any additional structure (like a topology). For $f\in \bbc^X$, we define the uniform norm

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{f}_\infty = \sup_{x\in X} |f(x)|, \end{equation}

allowing the possibility that $\norm{f}_\infty = \infty$. Notice that the set of all bounded $f\in \bbc^X$, i.e., those functions such that $\norm{f}_\infty<\infty$, is exactly the $\upC^\ast$-algebra $l^\infty(X)$ described in the previous section.

Theorem/Definition. Let $X$ be a nonempty set.

  1. The set $\bbc^X$ is a commutative, unital $\ast$-algebra with respect to the standard pointwise operations, including pointwise complex conjugation as the involution.

  2. For each $\dev>0$, define \begin{equation}\notag U_\dev = \{ (f,g) \in \bbc^X \times \bbc^X : \norm{f-g}_\infty <\dev\}. \end{equation} Then the $U_\dev$’s form a base for a uniformity on $\bbc^X$ called the uniformity of uniform convergence.

  3. For each $\dev>0$ and $f\in \bbc^X$, define \begin{equation}\notag B(f;\dev) = \{ g\in \bbc^X : \norm{f-g}_\infty<\dev \}. \end{equation} Then the uniform topology on $\bbc^X$ induced by the uniformity of uniform convergence has the $B(f;\dev)$’s as a neighborhood basis at $f$.

Exercise. Prove the theorem.

All the spaces of continuous functions in the display \eqref{cont-eqn} are subspaces of $\bbc^X$, and as we will see by the end of this section, they will all inherit various algebraic and topological structures from those of $\bbc^X$ listed in this Theorem/Definition. The reader might like to peek ahead at the summary to see where we are headed.

Before describing these spaces of continuous functions, let’s first investigate $\bbc^X$ a bit more:

Theorem. Let $X$ be a nonempty set and let $\bbc^X$ be equipped with the uniformity of uniform convergence and the associated uniform topology.

  1. The uniform space $\bbc^X$ is complete.

  2. The underlying additive abelian group of $\bbc^X$ is a topological group.

  3. The scaling operation \begin{equation}\notag \bbc \times \bbc^X \to \bbc^X, \quad (\lambda,f) \mapsto \lambda f, \end{equation} is continuous at any $(\lambda,f)$ for which $f$ is bounded.

(1.): The uniformity on $\bbc^X$ has a countable base, and thus it suffices to consider sequences instead of general nets for completeness. So, let $(f_n)_{n\geq 0}$ be a Cauchy sequence with respect to the uniformity. This means that for every $\dev>0$, there is an integer $N\geq 0$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag m,n\geq N \quad \Rightarrow \quad (f_m,f_n) \in U_\dev, \end{equation}

which is the same as

\begin{equation}\notag m,n\geq N \quad \Rightarrow \quad |f_m(x)-f_n(x)|<\dev, \end{equation}

for all $x\in X$. But this is exactly the familiar “Cauchy criterion for uniform convergence”, and thus there is a function $f:X \to \bbc$ such that $f_n \to f$ uniformly. Hence, $\bbc^X$ is complete.

(2.): We shall first prove that the addition map

\begin{equation}\notag \bbc^X \times \bbc^X \to \bbc^X, \quad (f,g) \mapsto f+g, \end{equation}

is continuous at a fixed point $(f_0,g_0)$. So, let $B(f_0+g_0;\dev)$ be a basic open neighborhood of $f_0+g_0$. Then the addition map sends a point

\begin{equation}\notag (f,g) \in B(f_0;\dev/2) \times B(g_0;\dev_2) \end{equation}

into $B(f_0+g_0;\dev)$ since

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{f+g - (f_0+g_0)}_\infty \leq \norm{f-f_0}_\infty + \norm{g-g_0}_\infty < \dev. \end{equation}

Thus, the addition map is continuous. It is even easier to see that the inversion map

\begin{equation}\notag \bbc^X \to \bbc^X, \quad f \mapsto -f, \end{equation}

is continuous, and hence $\bbc^X$ is a topological group.

(3.): Let $(\lambda_0,f_0)$ be a point in $\bbc \times \bbc^X$ for which $f_0$ is bounded, and let $B(\lambda_0f_0;\dev)$ be a basic open neighborhood of $\lambda_0f_0$. If $\delta_1,\delta_2>0$ and

\begin{equation}\notag (\lambda,f) \in \{\lambda : |\lambda-\lambda_0| < \delta_1 \} \times B(f_0;\delta_2), \end{equation}

then

\begin{align} |\lambda f(x) - \lambda_0f_0(x)| &\leq |\lambda f(x) - \lambda f_0(x) + \lambda f_0(x)- \lambda_0f_0(x)| \notag \\ &\leq |\lambda||f(x) - f_0(x)| + |\lambda - \lambda_0| |f_0(x)| \notag \\ &\leq (|\lambda_0| +\delta_2)\delta_1 + \delta_2|f_0(x)| \notag \end{align}

for all $x\in X$. Passing to supremums gives

\begin{equation}\label{mat-eqn} \norm{\lambda f - \lambda_0 f_0}_\infty \leq (|\lambda_0| +\delta_2)\delta_1 + \delta_2\norm{f_0}_\infty. \end{equation}

Thus, we may make the left-hand side of \eqref{mat-eqn} smaller than $\dev$ by choosing $\delta_1$ and $\delta_2$ sufficiently small. Hence, the scaling map is continuous at $(\lambda_0,f_0)$. Q.E.D.

Let’s now turn our attention toward the first space of continuous functions in the display \eqref{cont-eqn}. Here’s its definition:

Definition. Let $X$ be a topological space. We write $C(X)$ for the set of all continuous functions $f:X \to \bbc$.

Now, $C(X)$ is clearly a subset of $\bbc^X$ and it therefore inherits both a subspace uniformity and a subspace topology. The latter are quite familiar, of course, but let’s make sure that we are clear on the definition of the former:

Theorem/Definition. Let $X$ be a uniform space with uniformity $\calu$, and let $A$ be a subset of $X$.

  1. The subspace uniformity on $A$ is the uniformity $\calu_A$ having entourages of the form $U\cap (A\times A)$, for $U\in \calu$.

  2. The uniform topology on $A$ induced by $\calu_A$ is the same as the subspace topology that $A$ inherits from the uniform topology on $X$ induced by $\calu$.

Exercise. Prove the theorem. In particular, prove that $\calu_A$ really is a uniformity on $A$, and prove part (2.) in detail.

The next result will be used frequently in this section:

Theorem/Definition. Let $X$ be a uniform space and $A$ a subset of $X$.

  • We shall say $A$ is uniformly closed in $X$ if it is closed in the subspace topology inherited from the uniform topology on $X$ (which is the same as the uniform topology induced by the subspace uniformity).

If $X$ is a complete uniform space and $A$ is uniformly closed, then $A$ is a complete uniform space.

Exercise. Prove the theorem.

Now, we have:

Theorem. Let $X$ be a topological space. Then $C(X)$ is a uniformly closed $\ast$-subalgebra of $\bbc^X$.

Exercise. Prove the theorem.

Closely related to $C(X)$ is the second of the spaces in the display \eqref{cont-eqn}, which we now define:

Definition. Let $X$ be a topological space.

  • The support of a function $f:X \to \bbc$, denoted $\Supp{f}$, is the subset \begin{equation}\notag \Supp{f} = \overline{\{x\in X : f(x) \neq 0\}}, \end{equation} where the overline denotes topological closure.

  • We write $C(X;A)$ for the subset of $C(X)$ consisting of those continuous functions whose supports are contained in a fixed subset $A\subseteq X$.

Then:

Theorem. Let $X$ be a topological space and $A$ a subspace of $X$.

  1. The subset $C(X;A)$ of $C(X)$ is a $\ast$-subalgebra.

  2. If $A$ is a closed subspace, then $C(X;A)$ is uniformly closed in $C(X)$, and thus is complete.

Note that $C(X;A)$ does not contain the multiplicative identity unless $A = X$, so $C(X;A)$ will in general be a non-unital algebra.

(1.): To see that $C(X;A)$ is closed under addition, note that

\begin{equation}\label{huh-eqn} \{x\in X : (f+g)(x)\neq 0\} \subseteq \{x\in X : f(x)\neq 0\} \cup \{x\in X : g(x)\neq 0\} \end{equation}

if $f,g\in C(X;A)$. Passing to closures yields

\begin{equation}\notag \Supp{(f+g)} \subseteq \Supp{f} \cup \Supp{g} \subseteq A, \end{equation}

and hence $C(X;A)$ is closed under addition. A similar argument shows that it is also closed under multiplication, scaling, and the involution, and hence is a $\ast$-subalgebra of $C(X)$.

(2.): Now, suppose that $A$ is uniformly closed in $X$. Let $(f_n)_{n\geq 0}$ be a sequence in $C(X;A)$ that converges to a function $f \in C(X)$; we must prove that $f$ has support in $A$. Actually, we don’t need the full strength of uniform convergence in this situation, pointwise converge will suffice. Indeed, if $f(x)\neq 0$, then since

\begin{equation}\notag f(x) = \lim_{n\to \infty} f_n(x), \end{equation}

it must be the case that $f_n(x)\neq 0$ for all $n\gg 0$. In particular, we have

\begin{equation}\notag \{x \in X : f(x) \neq 0\} \subseteq \bigcup_{n\geq 0} \{x \in X : f_n(x) \neq 0\} \subseteq A \end{equation}

and passing to closures gives

\begin{equation}\notag \Supp{f} \subseteq \cl{A} = A. \end{equation}

Therefore, $C(X;A)$ is closed in $C(X)$, so it is complete. Q.E.D.

Neither $C(X)$ nor $C(X;A)$ are (in general) topological algebras, or even topological vector spaces. However, the third space in the display \eqref{cont-eqn} carries a norm, and thus it is a topological algebra.

Definition. Let $X$ be a topological space. We set

\begin{equation}\notag C_b(X) = C(X) \cap l^\infty(X). \end{equation}

Thus, $C_b(X)$ carries the uniform norm $\norm{\cdot}_\infty$ inherited from $l^\infty(X)$.

Of course, we have $C(X) = C_b(X) \subset l^\infty(X)$ if $X$ is a compact topological space. In any case, we always have:

Theorem. Let $X$ be a topological space. Then the subset $C_b(X)$ of $l^\infty(X)$ is a uniformly closed $\ast$-subalgebra, and is thus a commutative, unital $\upC^\ast$-algebra.

Exercise. Prove the theorem.

Now, the fourth space in \eqref{cont-eqn}:

Definition. Let $X$ be a topological space. We write $C_c(X)$ for the set of all continuous functions $f:X \to \bbc$ whose support is compact.

Clearly we have $C_c(X) \subseteq C_b(X)$ as subsets. Even more:

Theorem. Let $X$ be a topological space.

  1. The set $C_c(X)$ is a vector subspace of $C_b(X)$, and thus is itself a topological $\bbc$-vector space when equipped with the metric topology induced by the uniform norm.

  2. If $X$ is Hausdorff, then $C_c(X)$ is a $\ast$-subalgebra of $C_b(X)$.

Note that I am not claiming $C_c(X)$ is a $\upC^\ast$-subalgebra of $C_b(X)$ because, in general, it is not. The problem is that $C_c(X)$ does not have to be uniformly closed in $C_b(X)$.

(1.): I shall prove that $C_c(X)$ is closed under addition and leave the rest for you. As in \eqref{huh-eqn} above, given $f,g\in C_c(X)$, we have

\begin{equation}\notag \{x\in X : (f+g)(x)\neq 0\} \subseteq \{x\in X : f(x)\neq 0\} \cup \{x\in X : g(x)\neq 0\} \end{equation}

and hence

\begin{equation}\notag \Supp{(f+g)} \subseteq \Supp{f} \cup \Supp{g}. \end{equation}

Both of the sets on the right-hand side of this containment are compact, and hence so is their union. But then $\Supp{(f+g)}$ is a closed subset of a compact subspace of $X$ and is therefore itself compact in $X$.

(2.): It is clear that $C_c(X)$ is closed under the involution (this part does not need the Hausdorff condition), and so we need only prove that $C_c(X)$ is closed under multiplication. For this, given $f,g\in C_c(X)$, note that

\begin{equation}\notag \{x\in X : (fg)(x)\neq 0\} \subseteq \{x\in X : f(x)\neq 0\} \cap \{x\in X : g(x)\neq 0\}. \end{equation}

Passing to closures gives

\begin{equation}\notag \Supp{(fg)} \subseteq \Supp{f} \cap \Supp{g}. \end{equation}

Both of the sets on the right-hand side of this containment are compact, and since $X$ is Hausdorff, their intersection is compact too. Thus, $\Supp{(fg)}$ is a closed subset of a compact subspace of $X$ and is therefore itself compact in $X$. Q.E.D.

As I mentioned above, it is not the case that $C_c(X)$ is complete for arbitrary $X$; however, we can describe in a relatively concrete way its completion for spaces $X$ that are locally compact Hausdorff. This leads us to the fifth and final space in \eqref{cont-eqn}:

Definition. Let $X$ be a topological space.

  • A function $f:X \to \bbc$ is said to vanish at infinity if to every $\dev>0$, there is a compact subset $K \subseteq X$ such that $|f(x)|<\dev$ for all $x\notin K$.

  • The set of all continuous functions $f$ that vanish at infinity is denoted $C_0(X)$.

First, we prove:

Theorem. Let $X$ be a topological space.

  1. Every $f\in C_0(X)$ is bounded.

  2. The subset $C_0(X)$ is a $\upC^\ast$-subalgebra of $C_b(X)$.

(1.): Given $f\in C_0(X)$, there is a compact set $K$ such that $|f(x)|<1$ for all $x\notin K$. Hence, $f$ is bounded off $K$. But it is also bounded on $K$, since $f$ is continuous and the latter is compact. Thus, $f$ is bounded.

(2.): I will only prove that $C_0(X)$ is uniformly closed in $C_b(X)$, and let you handle the rest. So, let $(f_n)_{n\geq 0}$ be a sequence in $C_0(X)$ that converges uniformly to a function $f\in C_b(X)$. We need to prove that $f$ vanishes at infinity. So, let $\dev>0$ be given, and choose an integer $n\geq 0$ and a compact set $K$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag |f(x)-f_n(x)| < \dev/2 \quad \text{and} \quad |f_n(x)| < \dev/2 \end{equation}

for all $x\notin K$. But then

\begin{equation}\notag |f(x)| < |f_n(x)| + \dev/2 < \dev \end{equation}

for all $x\notin K$, and hence $f$ vanishes at infinity. This proves $C_0(X)$ is uniformly closed in $C_b(X)$. Q.E.D.

Then:

Theorem. Let $X$ be a locally compact Hausdorff space. Then $C_0(X)$ is the completion of $C_c(X)$ relative to the metric topology induced by the uniform norm.

We have already shown that $C_0(X)$ is complete in the metric topology induced by the uniform norm, so we need only prove that $C_c(X)$ is dense in $C_0(X)$. The proof of this latter fact uses a version of Urysohn’s lemma that produces “bump” type functions on compact sets in locally compact Hausdorff spaces. The precise statement is:

Urysohn’s Lemma for Locally Compact Hausdorff Spaces. Let $K \subseteq K \subseteq X$, where $X$ is a locally compact Hausdorff space, $K$ is open, and $K$ is compact. Then there exists $g\in C_c(X)$ with $0\leq g \leq 1$, $g=1$ on $K$, and $\Supp{g} \subseteq K$.

Now, suppose that we have a function $f\in C_0(X)$. For every integer $n\geq 1$, there exists a compact subset $K_n$ such that $|f(x)|\geq 1/n$ for all $x\in K_n$. Since $K_n$ is compact, there is a function $g_n\in C_c(X)$ such that $0\leq g_n \leq 1$ and $g_n = 1$ on $K_n$. If we define $f_n = g_nf$, then $f_n\in C_c(X)$ and $f_n\to f$ uniformly. Q.E.D.

Let’s summarize our results:

Summary. Let $X$ be a topological space. Except for $C(X;A)$, the other spaces of continuous functions in the display \eqref{cont-eqn} form a nested sequence of subspaces of the general function space $\bbc^X$:

\begin{equation}\notag C_c(X) \subseteq C_0(X) \subseteq C_b(X) \subseteq C(X) \subseteq \bbc^X. \end{equation}

Then:

  1. The set $\bbc^X$ carries an algebraic structure, in the form of a commutative, unital $\ast$-algebra, as well as a uniform/topological structure, in the form of the uniformity of uniform convergence. It is not, in general, a topological vector space.

  2. The space $C(X)$ is a uniformly closed $\ast$-subalgebra of $\bbc^X$, and is thus a $\ast$-algebra which is complete (but it is not, in general, a topological vector space).

  3. The space $C_b(X)$ is a uniformly closed $\ast$-subalgebra of $C(X)$, and it carries the uniform norm from the inclusion $C_b(X) \subseteq l^\infty(X)$. It is therefore a $\upC^\ast$-subalgebra of $l^\infty(X)$.

  4. The space $C_0(X)$ is a uniformly closed $\ast$-subalgebra of $C_b(X)$, and is thus a $\upC^\ast$-subalgebra of $C_b(X)$.

  5. Provided $X$ is a Hausdorff space, the space $C_c(X)$ is a topological $\ast$-subalgebra of $C_0(X)$. Provided that $X$ is also locally compact, the $\upC^\ast$-algebra $C_0(X)$ is the completion of $C_c(X)$.

Continuous functions and integration

So far, the only connection we have discovered between spaces of continuous functions on a topological space $X$ and the $L^p$-spaces discussed in the previous section goes through the space $l^\infty(X)$ of bounded functions. Remember that $l^\infty(X)$ really stands for $L^\infty(X,\mfP(X),\nu)$, where $\nu$ is the counting measure and $\mfP(X)$ is the power set.

The counting measure, of course, is defined on any set whatsoever, and does not depend on the topological structure of $X$. However, recall from your course on measure and integration that many interesting and useful things can be said about certain types of measures that interact coherently with topologies through the associated Borel algebras; here, by “interact coherently,” I mean that such a measure is one of the following type:

Definition. Let $X$ be a locally compact Hausdorff space. A Borel measure $\mu$ on $X$ is called a Radon measure if it has the following properties:

  1. We have $\mu(K) <\infty$ for all compact subsets $K\subseteq X$.

  2. Outer regularity. For all Borel sets $A$, we have \begin{equation}\notag \mu(A) = \inf \{ \mu(U) : \text{$U$ open and $U\supseteq A$}\}. \end{equation}

  3. Inner regularity. For all open subsets $U$, we have \begin{equation}\notag \mu(U) = \sup \{ \mu(K) : \text{$K$ compact and $K\subseteq U$}\}. \end{equation}

The Lebesgue measure on $\bbr^n$, for instance, is a Radon measure. In fact, one can construct the Lebesgue measure

Theorem. Let $\mu$ be a Radon measure on a locally compact Hausdorff space $X$. For $1\leq p < \infty$, the space $L^p(X,\mu)$ is the completion of $C_c(X)$ relative to the metric topology induced by the $L^p$-norm.

Blah!

Interlude: locally convex spaces

Having discussed spaces of continuous and integrable functions, we now transition toward differentiable functions. In particular, spaces of infinitely differentiable functions (or smooth functions) will be one of the primary types of function spaces that we will study. But there’s a problem: Many spaces of smooth functions have natural and useful topologies that are not normed, but are instead generated by families of seminorms. We study these types of topologies in this section.

Let’s begin with a simple example that will (hopefully) motivate the abstract definitions. Let $[a,b]$ be a nonempty closed interval in $\bbr$, let $n\geq 1$ be an integer, and let $C^n([a,b])$ be the $\bbc$-vector space of all functions $f:[a,b]\to \bbc$ that posses continuous derivatives of all orders $\leq n$. In particular, a function $f\in C^n([a,b])$ and all its derivatives are necessarily bounded on $[a,b]$, and hence the number

\begin{equation}\label{sum-norm-eqn} \norm{f} \defeq \norm{f}_\infty + \norm{f’}_\infty + \cdots + \norm{f^{(n)}}_\infty \end{equation}

is finite, where $\norm{\cdot}_\infty$ denotes the uniform norm. Now, it turns out that this definition of $\norm{\cdot}$ yields a norm on $C^n([a,b])$, and that we even obtain a Banach space; however, this same topology may be generated in a slightly different manner by first decomposing the norm \eqref{sum-norm-eqn} into its “components” given by

\begin{equation}\label{semi-norm-eqn} \norm{f}_k \defeq \norm{f^{(k)}}_\infty, \end{equation}

where we take $f^{(0)} =f$ in the case $k=0$.

Theorem. For each integer $k=0,1,\ldots,n$, all real numbers $\dev>0$, and every function $f_0 \in C^n([a,b])$, define

\begin{equation}\notag B_k(f_0;\dev) = \{ f\in C^n([a,b]) : \norm{f-f_0}_k < \dev\}. \end{equation}

Then there is a unique topology on $C^n([a,b])$ for which the neighborhood system at $f_0$ has a base given by all intersections of the form

\begin{equation}\notag B_0(f_0;\dev_0) \cap \cdots \cap B_n(f_0;\dev_n), \end{equation}

where $\dev_0,\dev_1,\ldots,\dev_n>0$. With respect to this topology the space $C^n([a,b])$ is a topological vector space; in fact, this topology is the same as the topology induced by the norm \eqref{sum-norm-eqn}.

Thus, the topology on $C^n([a,b])$ generated by the norm \eqref{sum-norm-eqn} may instead be generated by the family of real-valued functions \eqref{semi-norm-eqn} in the manner described in the theorem. Notice that except for $\norm{\cdot}_0 = \norm{\cdot}_\infty$, these functions are not norms on $C^n([a,b])$ since they are only positive indefinite, and not positive definite. In other words, each $\norm{\cdot}_k$ is a seminorm.

Now, suppose that we let $n\to \infty$, and that we aim to topologize the space

\begin{equation}\notag C^\infty([a,b]) = \bigcap_{n\geq 1} C^n([a,b]) \end{equation}

of smooth functions on $[a,b]$. At first, one might naively hope that the formula \eqref{sum-norm-eqn} extends to yield a norm

\begin{equation}\label{bad-norm-eqn} \norm{f} = \sum_{k= 0}^\infty \norm{f}_k, \end{equation}

but this clearly doesn’t work since the sum does not converge in general. Rather, in order to topologize $C^\infty([a,b])$, we might instead use the seminorms \eqref{semi-norm-eqn} individually, and attempt to generate a topology on $C^\infty([a,b])$ using the trick in the previous theorem. This turns out to work!

The procedure to accomplish this is the general one outlined in the following definition.

Definition. Let $\{\norm{\cdot}_{\lambda}\}_{\lambda\in \Lambda}$ be a collection of seminorms on a vector space $\calv$, indexed by an arbitrary set $\Lambda$. Then the locally convex topology on $\calv$ generated by these seminorms is the coarest topology with the following pair of properties:

  1. The topology is homogeneous, i.e., for each $v\in \calv$, the left-translation map \begin{equation}\notag L_v: \calv\to \calv, \quad w\mapsto v+w, \end{equation} is a self-homeomorphism.

  2. Each seminorm $\norm{\cdot}_\lambda:\calv \to \bbr$ is continuous at $0\in V$.

A locally convex space is a vector space $\calv$ equipped with a locally convex topology generated by a collection of seminorms.

Clearly the locally convex topology exists, since it is the intersection of all topologies on $\calv$ that have the two stated properties (and at least one such topology exists, namely the discrete one). But we can actually do better than this abstract existence proof; we can actually describe neighborhood bases quite concretely.

We imagine building up the locally convex topology $\calt$ starting from the indiscrete topology $\calt = \{\emptyset,\calv\}$. We add just those sets to $\calt$ that are forced upon us by the two conditions (1.) and (2.) in the statement of the definition, and then we stop—we add no more sets! This is because $\calt$ is supposed to be the coarsest topology with these two properties.

Now, since each seminorm is supposed to be continuous at $0\in \calv$, for each $\dev>0$ and each $\lambda \in \Lambda$, the set

\begin{equation}\label{yeah-eqn} B_\lambda(0;\dev) \defeq \{ v\in \calv : \norm{v}_\lambda < \dev\} \end{equation}

must be in $\calt$, since this set is the preimage under the seminorm $\norm{\cdot}_\lambda$ of the open interval $(-\dev,\dev)$ in $\bbr$. Once we add the $B_\lambda(0;\dev)$’s to $\calt$, then $\calt$ automatically contains all finite intersections of the form

\begin{equation}\notag B_{\lambda_1}(0;\dev_1) \cap \cdots \cap B_{\lambda_n}(0;\dev_n) \end{equation}

because $\calt$ is supposed to be closed under finite intersections. (It also contains all arbitrary unions of the $B_\lambda(0;\dev)$’s.)

The left-translation maps are supposed to be self-homeomorphisms, and hence the sets

\begin{equation}\notag B_\lambda(v_0;\dev) \defeq L_{v_0}(B_\lambda(0;\dev)) = \{ v\in \calv : \norm{v-v_0}_\lambda < \dev \} \end{equation}

must also be in $\calt$, for every $v_0\in \calv$, $\lambda \in \lambda$, and $\dev>0$. As above, $\calt$ then also contains all finite intersections

\begin{equation}\label{fi-eqn} B_{\lambda_1}(v_0;\dev_1) \cap \cdots \cap B_{\lambda_n}(v_0;\dev_n). \end{equation}

Moreover, if we stop adding sets to $\calt$ here, then we’ve obtained a topology for which finite intersections of the form \eqref{fi-eqn} give a neighborhood base at $v_0$.

Ok sure, if we stop now, then $\calt$ has nice neighborhood bases—but does it have the two required properties (1.) and (2.)? Let’s check them:

  1. We need only check that left-translation $L_{v}$ is continuous. (Why?) For this, let $v_0\in \calv$ be given, and choose a subbasis open set $B_\lambda(v+v_0;\dev)$ containing $L_{v}(v_0) = v+v_0$. Then \begin{equation}\label{who-eqn} L_{v}\left(B_\lambda(v_0;\dev) \right) \subseteq B_\lambda(v+v_0;\dev). \end{equation} Indeed, if $w\in B_\lambda(v_0;\dev)$, then $\norm{w-v_0}_\lambda <\dev$, and so \begin{equation}\notag \norm{L_{v}(w) - (v + v_0)}_\lambda = \norm{w-v_0}_\lambda < \dev, \end{equation} which proves \eqref{who-eqn}.

  2. That each seminorm $\norm{\cdot}_\lambda$ is continuous at $0\in \calv$ follows immediately from the fact that $\calt$ contains all sets of the form \eqref{yeah-eqn}.

Great! Let’s summarize our findings (along with a few other properties of the locally convex topology) in a theorem:

Theorem. Let $\calv$ be a locally convex space with topology generated by a family of seminorms $\{\norm{\cdot}_\lambda\}_{\lambda\in \Lambda}$.

  1. If for each vector $v_0\in \calv$, each index $\lambda\in \Lambda$, and every real number $\dev>0$ we define \begin{equation}\notag B_\lambda(v_0;\dev) = \{ v\in \calv : \norm{v-v_0}_\lambda < \dev\}, \end{equation} then a neighborhood base at $v_0$ is given by all finite intersections of the form \begin{equation}\notag B_{\lambda_1}(v_0;\dev_1) \cap \cdots \cap B_{\lambda_n}(v_0;\dev_n). \end{equation}

  2. Equipped with this topology, $\calv$ is a topological vector space.

  3. Each seminorm $\norm{\cdot}_\lambda$ is continuous everywhere, not just at $0\in \calv$.

  4. The locally convex topology is Hausdorff if and only if for every nonzero vector $v\in \calv$, there is a $\lambda \in \Lambda$ such that $\norm{v}_\lambda\neq 0$.

We’ve already established (1.), so let’s move on to (2.): Since the locally convex topology is homogeneous, we need only show that the subtraction map

\begin{equation}\notag V \times V \to V, \quad (v,w) \mapsto v-w \end{equation}

is continuous at $(0,0)$, and that the scaling map

\begin{equation}\notag \bbc \times \calv \to \calv, \quad (\lambda,v) \mapsto \lambda v \end{equation}

is also continuous at $(0,0)$. For the first, let $B_\lambda(0;\dev)$ be a subbasic open neighborhood of $0\in \calv$. But then the subtraction and scaling maps carry the product open sets

\begin{equation}\notag B_\lambda(0;\dev/2) \times B_\lambda(0;\dev/2) \subset \calv \times \calv \end{equation}

and

\begin{equation}\notag \{\lambda \in \bbc : |\lambda| <1 \} \times B_\lambda(0;\dev) \subset \bbc \times \calv \end{equation}

into $B_\lambda(0;\dev)$.

(3.): We must prove that $\norm{\cdot}_\lambda$ is continuous at a fixed vector $v_0\in V$. But this is easy, since for any $\dev>0$, the triangle inequality implies

\begin{equation}\notag |\norm{v}_\lambda - \norm{v_0}_\lambda| \leq \norm{v-v_0}_\lambda < \dev. \end{equation}

Thus, $\norm{\cdot}_\lambda$ is indeed continuous at $v_0$.

(4.): If it were the case that $v\neq 0$ and $\norm{v}_\lambda =0$ for all $\lambda \in \Lambda$, then the zero vector $0$ is contained in every $B_\lambda(v;\dev)$. Thus, there are no disjoint open sets that separate $0$ and $v$, and so the topology is not Hausdorff. Conversely, suppose that $v$ and $w$ are distinct vectors in $V$. Then there is a $\lambda$ such that $\dev = \norm{v-w}_\lambda \neq 0$, and then

\begin{equation}\notag B_\lambda(v;\dev/2) \cap B_\lambda(w;\dev/2) = \emptyset, \end{equation}

which proves the topology is Hausdorff. Q.E.D.

Returning to $C^\infty([a,b])$, we see that it may be equipped with the locally convex topology generated by the family of seminorms $\{\norm{\cdot}_k\}_{k\geq 0}$ given by

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{f}_k = \norm{f^{(k)}}_{\infty}. \end{equation}

This topology is very tightly linked with the topologies on $C^n([a,b])$ as the latter are also locally convex, but generated by finite families of seminorms. A good definition should always have this unifying effect.

We’ve also convinced ourselves that the locally convex topology on $C^\infty([a,b])$ is not induced by the naive norm \eqref{bad-norm-eqn}, primarily due to the fact that the latter isn’t actually a norm! But it’s not obvious that there isn’t a norm somewhere out in the aether that induces this topology, and that we just haven’t found it. This turns out not to be the case—the general theory says that no such norm exists, and so we really have left the familiar world of normed vector spaces. I will not prove this here, but instead refer you to an argument given in Section 1 of Rudin’s Functional Analysis (page 34, to be precise) which may be adapted to prove $C^\infty([a,b])$ is not normable.

Let’s now turn our attention toward a question that may have occurred to you: Why the name “locally convex?” After all, at least on first reading, there doesn’t seem to be anything particularly “convex” about topologies defined by families of seminorms. Before investigating, let’s make sure we agree on terminology:

Definition. Let $A$ be a subset of a vector space $V$. The set $A$ is called …

  1. … convex if it contains the line segment \begin{equation}\notag [v,w] = \{ tv + (1-t)w : 0 \leq t \leq 1\} \end{equation} for all $v,w\in A$.

  2. … balanced if $\lambda A \subseteq A$ whenever $\lambda$ is a scalar with $|\lambda| \leq 1$.

Notice that a balanced subset necessarily contains the zero vector $0\in V$. These types of subsets occur naturally in topological vector spaces:

Theorem. Let $\calv$ be a topological vector space. Then $0\in \calv$ has a neighborhood basis consisting of balanced open subsets.

For the proof, let $N$ be a neighborhood of $0$. Since the scaling map

\begin{equation}\notag \bbc \times \calv \to \calv, \quad (\lambda,v) \mapsto \lambda v, \end{equation}

is continuous, there is an $\dev>0$ and an open neighborhood $U$ of $0$ such that $\lambda v \in N$, provided that $|\lambda|\leq\dev$ and $v\in U$. For fixed $\lambda$, notice that the set $\lambda U$ is open in $\calv$; indeed, this is because this set is the image of the open set $U$ under the map

\begin{equation}\notag \calv \to \calv, \quad v \mapsto \lambda v, \end{equation}

which is a homeomorphism (why?). Thus, the set

\begin{equation}\notag B = \bigcup_{|\lambda|\leq \dev} \lambda U \end{equation}

is open in $\calv$ and is contained in $N$. I claim that $B$ is balanced. To see this, let $\mu\in \bbc$ with $|\mu|\leq 1$. Then

\begin{equation}\notag \mu B = \bigcup_{|\lambda|\leq \dev} (\mu \lambda) U \subseteq B \end{equation}

since $|\mu\lambda| \leq \dev$. We have therefore shown that an arbitrary neighborhood $N$ of $0$ contains a balanced, open neighborhood $B$. Q.E.D.

So, the zero vector in every topological vector space has a neighborhood basis consisting of balanced open subsets. It turns out that what distinguishes a generic topological vector space from a locally convex one is that the zero vectors in the latter have neighborhood bases consisting of balanced open subsets that are also convex. This explains the terminology locally convex.

One direction of the proof of this characterization of locally convex spaces is easy. Indeed, if $V$ is a locally convex space with generating seminorms $\{\norm{\cdot}_\lambda\}$, notice that the finite intersection

\begin{equation}\notag B_{\lambda_1}(0;\dev_1) \cap \cdots \cap B_{\lambda_n}(0;\dev_n) \end{equation}

is convex, balanced, and open: If $v$ and $w$ are two vectors in this intersection and $0 \leq t \leq 1$, then

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{tv + (1-t)w}_{\lambda_i} \leq t \norm{v}_{\lambda_i} + (1-t) \norm{w}_{\lambda_i} < \dev_i \end{equation}

for each $i=1,\ldots,n$, and hence the intersection is convex. Also, if $|\lambda| \leq 1$, then

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{\lambda v}_{\lambda_i} = |\lambda| \norm{v}_{\lambda_i} <\dev, \end{equation}

so the intersection is also balanced.

In order to obtain the other half of the proof of the characterization of locally convex spaces, we need the following fundamental link between seminorms and convexity:

Theorem. Let $U$ be a nonempty open set in a topological vector space $V$. If $U$ is convex and balanced, then the function

\begin{equation}\label{set-eqn} v\mapsto \norm{v}_U \defeq \inf\{t : t\geq 0, \ v\in tU\} \end{equation}

is a seminorm on $V$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag U = \{ v \in V : \norm{v}_U < 1 \}. \end{equation}

The (necessarily unique) seminorm $\norm{\cdot}_U$ is called the Minkowski functional associated to $U$.

Openness of $U$ may be used to show that the infimum in \eqref{set-eqn} is a finite number, since the set $\{t : t\geq 0, \ v\in tU\}$ is necessarily nonempty for each $v\in V$. The balanced property of $U$ is used to prove that the seminorm is homogeneous, and convexity is used to show that it satisfies the triangle inequality. I will leave you to work out the precise details on your own, or to look them up (take a look at Chapter IV in Conway’s book mentioned in the References).

Now, here’s the advertised characterization of locally convex spaces:

Theorem. A topological vector space $V$ is locally convex if and only if the collection $\calb$ of all convex and balanced open sets forms a neighborhood base at $0$.

In particular, if the condition holds, then the topology on $V$ is the locally convex one induced by the family of seminorms $\{\norm{\cdot}_U\}_{U\in \calb}$, where $\norm{\cdot}_U$ is the Minkowski functional associated with $U$.

We have already argued that the condition is necessary in order for $\calv$ to be locally convex, so we must only prove sufficiency. To do this, we shall first prove that $\calb$ consists exactly of those finite intersections of the form

\begin{equation}\label{blind-eqn} B_{U_1}(0;\dev_1) \cap \cdots \cap B_{U_n}(0;\dev_n), \end{equation}

where, as usual, we define

\begin{equation}\notag B_{U_i}(0;\dev_i) = \{ v\in V : \norm{v}_{U_i} < \dev_i\}. \end{equation}

First, note that if $U$ is a convex and balanced open set, then

\begin{equation}\notag B_U(0;1) = \{ v\in V : \norm{v}_U < 1\} = U, \end{equation}

and thus every $U\in \calb$ is of the form \eqref{blind-eqn}. On the other hand, one easily proves that every set of the form \eqref{blind-eqn} is convex and balanced (we did this above), so we need only prove that it is open to conclude that it is contained in $\calb$. For this, note that since $V$ is a topological vector space, each scaling map

\begin{equation}\notag S_{\dev_i}: V\to V, \quad v\mapsto \dev_i v, \end{equation}

is a self-homeomorphism. Thus, since $U_i$ is open, so too is $\dev_i U_i$. But this latter set is exactly $B_{U_i}(0;\dev_i)$, and thus \eqref{blind-eqn} is a finite intersection of open sets.

We have therefore shown that $0\in V$ has a neighborhood basis consisting of all sets of the form \eqref{blind-eqn}. Since the topology on $V$ is homogeneous, this means that every vector $v_0\in V$ has a neighborhood basis of the form

\begin{equation}\notag B_{U_1}(v_0;\dev_1) \cap \cdots \cap B_{U_n}(v_0;\dev_n). \end{equation}

But this means the topology is exactly the locally convex topology generated by the seminorms $\{\norm{\cdot}_U\}_{U\in \calb}$, as claimed. Q.E.D.

Completeness of locally convex spaces will be quite important in what follows, so let’s talk briefly about the relation between uniform spaces and locally convex ones. We know that every locally convex space $\calv$ is a topological vector space and hence also a uniform space. Indeed, a basis for the uniformity is given by all sets of the form

\begin{equation}\notag U_N = \{ (v,w) \in \calv \times \calv : v-w \in N\} \end{equation}

as $N$ ranges through a neighborhood basis of $0\in \calv$. We explored all this in our earlier post on uniform spaces.

Every seminorm $\norm{\cdot}$ on a vector space $\calv$ induces a pseudometric $d$ via the formula

\begin{equation}\notag d(v,w) = \norm{v-w}, \quad v,w\in \calv. \end{equation}

The seminorm origin of $d$ is reflected in the following two properties:

  1. Homogeneity: For all $\lambda \in \bbc$ and $v,w\in \calv$, we have \begin{equation}\notag d(\lambda v, \lambda w) = |\lambda| d(v,w). \end{equation}

  2. Translation invariance: For all $u,v,w\in \calv$, we have \begin{equation}\notag d(u+v,w+v) = d(u,w). \end{equation}

Conversely, suppose that $d$ is a pseudometric on $\calv$ with these two properties; if for each $v\in \calv$ we set

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{v} = d(v,0), \end{equation}

then $\norm{\cdot}$ is a seminorm on $\calv$. Indeed, homogeneity of $\norm{\cdot}$ follows from the same property of $d$, while the triangle inequality follows from the same property of $d$ and translation invariance:

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{v+w} = d(v+w,0) \leq d(v+w,w) + d(w,0) = d(v,0) + d(w,0) = \norm{v} + \norm{w}. \end{equation}

Thus, every seminorm on $\calv$ determines a homogeneous, translation invariant pseudometric, and vice versa.

We learned in the first post of this series that a single pseudometric $d$ on a set $X$ induces a pseudometric uniformity $\calu$ with basis given by those sets of the form

\begin{equation}\notag U_\dev = \{ (x,y) \in X \times X : d(x,y) < \dev\}, \end{equation}

for $\dev>0$. This construction may be generalized to a family of pseudometrics:

Theorem. Let $\{d_\lambda\}_{\lambda \in \Lambda}$ be a collection of pseudometrics on a set $X$, indexed by an arbitrary set $\Lambda$. Then there is a unique coarsest uniformity $\calu$ on $X$ such that for each $\lambda\in \Lambda$ the map

\begin{equation}\notag \iota_\lambda: (X,\calu) \to (X,\calu_\lambda), \quad x\mapsto x, \end{equation}

is uniformly continuous, where $\calu_\lambda$ is the uniformity on $X$ induced by the single metric $d_\lambda$.

  1. A base for the uniformity $\calu$ is given by all finite intersections of the form \begin{equation}\notag U_{\lambda_1,\dev_1} \cap \cdots \cap U_{\lambda_n,\dev_n}, \end{equation} where for each $\lambda\in \Lambda$ and $\dev>0$ we define \begin{equation}\notag U_{\lambda,\dev} = \{(x,y) \in X \times X : d_\lambda(x,y) < \dev \}. \end{equation}

  2. A net $(x_i)_{i\in I}$ in $X$ is Cauchy with respect to $\calu$ if and only if it is Cauchy with respect to all uniformities $\calu_\lambda$.

  3. If a net $(x_i)_{i\in I}$ converges to $x\in X$ with respect to $\calu$, then it also converges to $x$ with respect to each $\calu_\lambda$.

Exercise. Prove the theorem.

Now, let’s put it all together. We have a vector space $\calv$ with a family of seminorms $\{\norm{\cdot}_\lambda\}_{\lambda\in \Lambda}$. These seminorms induce a locally convex topology on $\calv$, which in turn gives $\calv$ the structure of a topological vector space. A basis for $0\in \calv$ in this topology is given by all finite intersections of the form

\begin{equation}\label{unif-eqn} B_{\lambda_1}(0;\dev_1) \cap \cdots \cap B_{\lambda_n}(0;\dev_n). \end{equation}

On the other hand, the seminorms induce a family of pseudometrics $\{d_\lambda\}_{\lambda\in \Lambda}$ with

\begin{equation}\notag d_\lambda(v,w) = \norm{v-w}_\lambda. \end{equation}

These pseudometrics induce a uniformity on $\calv$, which in turn induces a uniform topology on $\calv$ with respect to which $0\in \calv$ has a neighborhood basis given exactly by the same intersections \eqref{unif-eqn}, as you may check. Thus, the uniform topology induced by the pseudometrics is the same as the locally convex topology induced by the seminorms. The criterion for net convergence in the previous theorem will be particularly useful when we discuss specific examples of complete locally convex spaces in the next section.

Exercise. Let $\calv$ be a locally convex space whose topology generated by the family of seminorms $\{\norm{\cdot}_\lambda\}$. We proved above that each seminorm is continuous at $0\in \calv$. Now, with respect to the pseudometric uniformity on $\calv$ described above, prove that each seminorm $\norm{\cdot}_\lambda$ is uniformly continuous.

One particular class of complete locally convex spaces is singled out for attention:

Definition. A locally convex space $\calv$ is called a Fréchet space if it is Hausdorff and complete, and if its topology is defined by a countable family of seminorms $\{\norm{\cdot}_k\}_{k\geq 0}$.

It is a general result in the theory of uniform spaces that the uniform topology induced by a pseudometric uniformity generated by a countable family of pseudometrics is automatically metrizable. Thus, Fréchet spaces are particular types of complete, Hausdorff metric spaces.

An important class of Fréchet spaces will be studied in the next section. We will also study a certain type of locally convex space that is not itself a Fréchet space, but is rather a directed colimit of Fréchet subspaces. Here’s the general procedure:

Theorem. Let $\calv$ be a vector space with a collection $\Sigma$ of vector subspaces such that:

  1. Each $A\in \Sigma$ is a locally convex space.

  2. The collection $\Sigma$ is directed by inclusion, and all inclusion maps $A\to A’$ between subspaces in $\Sigma$ (whenever defined) are continuous.

  3. We have $\calv = \bigcup_{A\in \Sigma} A$.

Then there is a unique structure of locally convex space on $\calv$ for which the latter is the (directed) colimit over $\Sigma$ in the category of locally convex spaces:

\begin{equation}\notag \calv = \colim_{A\in \Sigma} A. \end{equation}

In particular, a neighborhood base at $0\in \calv$ is given by all convex and balanced subsets of $\calv$ whose intersections with each $A\in \Sigma$ are open in $A$.

Exercise. Prove the theorem.

Finally:

Definition. Let the notation be as in the theorem immediately above. If the subspaces $A\in \Sigma$ are Fréchet spaces, and if $\Sigma$ is countable, then $\calv$ equipped with the locally convex colimit topology described in the theorem is called an $LF$-space.

The “$LF$” stands for Limit Fréchet.

Spaces of differentiable functions

Definition. Let $U$ be a nonempty open subset of $\bbr^d$.

  • We define $C^n(U)$ to be the set of all functions $f:U\to \bbc$ whose partial derivatives of orders $\leq n$ exist and are continuous. We then define \begin{equation}\notag C^\infty(U) = \bigcap_{n\geq 0} C^n(U). \end{equation} We identify $C^0(U)$ with $C(U)$. Functions in $C^n(U)$, including the case $n=\infty$, are said to be $C^n$-functions, or functions of class $C^n$. Functions in $C^\infty(U)$ are also called smooth.

  • For each $n\geq 0$, including $n=\infty$, we define \begin{equation}\notag C^n_c(U) = C^n(U) \cap C_c(U). \end{equation}

  • Let $A$ be a subset of $U$. For each $n\geq 0$, including $n=\infty$, we define $C^n(U;A)$ (respectively, $C^n_c(U;A)$) to be those functions $f\in C^n(U)$ (respectively, $C^n_c(U)$) with support contained in $A$.

Blah!

Theorem. Let $U$ be a nonempty open subset of $\bbr^m$ and $K$ a compact subset of $U$.

  1. We have \begin{equation}\notag C^n(U;K) = C^n(\bbr^m;K) \end{equation} for $0 \leq n \leq \infty$.

  2. We have that $C(\bbr^m;K)$ is a Banach subspace of $C_b(\bbr^m)$ equipped with the uniform norm.

Blah!

Theorem. Let $U$ be a nonempty open subset of the real line $\bbr$ and let $K$ be a compact subset of $U$. Then $C^\infty(U;K) = C^\infty(\bbr;K)$ is a Fréchet space with respect to the collection of seminorms $\{\norm{\cdot}_k\}_{k\geq 0}$ with

\begin{equation}\label{leaf-eqn} \norm{f}_k = \norm{f^{(k)}}_\infty. \end{equation}

We need to prove that the locally convex topology generated by the (countably many) seminorms is both Hausdorff and complete.

To establish the Hausdorff property, let $f$ be a nonzero function in $C^\infty(\bbr;K)$. Choose two numbers $s$ and $t$ in $\bbr$ such that $f(s)\neq 0$ and $f(t) = 0$. By the Mean Value Theorem, there is a number $c$ strictly between $s$ and $t$ such that

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{f}_1 \geq |f’(c)| = \frac{|f(s)-f(t)|}{|s-t|} \neq 0. \end{equation}

Since $f$ was an arbitrarily chosen nonzero function and it has at least one non-vanishing seminorm, we conclude that the topology is Hausdorff.

Now let $(f_n)_{n\geq 0}$ be a sequence in $C^\infty(\bbr;K)$ which is Cauchy with respect to the locally convex topology generated by the seminorms \eqref{leaf-eqn}. We saw above that this means the sequence is also Cauchy with respect to each of the pseudometrics

\begin{equation}\notag d_k(f,g) \defeq \norm{f-g}_k = \norm{f^{(k)} - g^{(k)}}_\infty. \end{equation}

Therefore, each sequence $(f^{(k)}_n)_{n\geq 0}$ is Cauchy in the Banach space $C(\bbr;K)$ equipped with the uniform norm, which means that for each $k\geq 0$ there is a $g_k\in C(\bbr;K)$ such that $f^{(k)}_n \to g_k$ uniformly.

In particular, we have $f_n \to f$ uniformly, where for notational consistency we write $f$ for $g_0$. I claim that $f\in C^\infty(\bbr;K)$, and that the convergence $f_n\to f$ also holds with respect to the locally convex topology.

I will first prove that $f$ is differentiable and $f’ = g_1$. Choose a closed interval $[a,b]$ in $\bbr$ containing $K$. Since $f’_n \to g_1$ uniformly and each $f_n’$ is Riemann integrable, for every $x\in [a,b]$ we have

\begin{equation}\notag \int_a^x g_1(t) \ \d t = \lim_{n\to \infty} \int_a^x f’_n(t) \ \d t. \end{equation}

But by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, we have

\begin{equation}\notag f_n(x) - f_n(a) = \int_a^x f’_n(t) \ \d t, \end{equation}

and so

\begin{equation}\notag f(x) - f(a) = \int_a^x g_1(t) \ \d t. \end{equation}

In particular, since $g_1$ is continuous, the function $f$ is differentiable and $f’(x) = g_1(x)$ for all $x\in [a,b]$. In fact, since $K \subseteq [a,b]$ and the supports of both $f’$ and $g_1$ are contained in $K$, we must have $f’ = g_1$.

Now, replacing $f_n$ with $f_n^{(k)}$ for some $k\geq 0$, and $f_n’$ with $f_n^{(k+1)}$, the same argument as above shows $g_k’ = g_{k+1}$. Thus, $g_k = f^{(k)}$ for each $k\geq 0$, and hence $f^{(k)}_n \to f^{(k)}$ uniformly. But this means that $f_n \to f$ in the locally convex topology, which is what we wanted to prove. Q.E.D.

Theorem. Let $U$ be a nonempty open subset of $\bbr^m$ and let $K$ be a compact subset of $U$. Then $C^\infty(U;K)=C^\infty(\bbr^m;K)$ is a Fréchet space with respect to the collection of seminorms $\{ \norm{\cdot}_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in \bbn^m}$ with

\begin{equation} \label{par-der-eqn} \norm{f}_{\alpha} = \norm{ \bd^\alpha f}_\infty. \end{equation}

We shall prove the theorem only when $m=2$; all the essential ideas are contained in this special case, the general case is just more notationally cumbersome.

We first define a collection of auxiliary maps. For fixed $y\in \bbr$, define

\begin{equation}\notag \dev_y: C(\bbr^2;K) \to C(\bbr;K_y), \quad f\mapsto \dev_yf, \end{equation}

where $\dev_yf(x) = f(x,y)$ for each $x\in \bbr$ and

\begin{equation}\notag K_y = \{ x\in \bbr : (x,y) \in K\}. \end{equation}

Similarly, for fixed $x\in \bbr$, we define

\begin{equation}\notag \dev^x: C(\bbr^2;K) \to C(\bbr;K^x), \quad f\mapsto \dev^xf, \end{equation}

where $\dev^xf(y) = f(x,y)$ for each $y\in \bbr$ and

\begin{equation}\notag K^x = \{ y\in \bbr : (x,y) \in K\}. \end{equation}

Note that the sets $K_y$ and $K^x$ are compact in $\bbr$, and that the domains and codomains of the maps $\dev_y$ and $\dev^x$ are Banach spaces with respect to the uniform norms. It is not difficult to show that these maps are continuous with respect to these norms, and thus they preserve the limits of convergent sequences. They also commute with derivatives:

\begin{equation}\label{def-par-eqn} \dev_y(\bd^{(n,0)}f) = \frac{\d^n}{\d x^n}( \dev_y f) \quad \text{and} \quad \dev^x(\bd^{(0,n)}f) = \frac{\d^n}{\d y^n} (\dev^xf). \end{equation}

Indeed, these are essentially the definitions of partial derivatives.

Now, let $(f_n)_{n\geq 0}$ be a Cauchy sequence in $C^\infty(\bbr^2;K)$ with respect to the locally convex topology generated by the seminorms \eqref{par-der-eqn}. For each $\alpha \in \bbn^2$, the sequence $(\bd^\alpha f_n)_{n\geq 0}$ is then Cauchy in the uniform metric on $C(\bbr^2;K)$; thus, we have functions $g_\alpha \in C(\bbr^2;K)$ such that $\bd^\alpha f_n \to g_\alpha$ uniformly.

In particular, we have $f_n \to f$ uniformly, where for notational brevity and consistency we set $f = g_{(0,0)}$. I will show that the two first-order partial derivatives of the limit function $f$ exist, and that

\begin{equation}\notag \bd^{(1,0)}f = g_{(1,0)} \quad \text{and} \quad \bd^{(0,1)}f = g_{(0,1)}. \end{equation}

For fixed $y\in \bbr$, the function $\dev_y$ is continuous; therefore, we have

\begin{equation}\label{single1-eqn} \dev_y f_n \to \dev_y f \quad \text{and} \quad \dev_y(\bd^{(1,0)}f_n) \to \dev_y g_{(1,0)} \end{equation}

uniformly. But from \eqref{def-par-eqn}, we get that

\begin{equation}\label{single2-eqn} \frac{\d}{\d x}(\dev_y f_n) \to \dev_y g_{(1,0)} \end{equation}

uniformly. Now, the functions in \eqref{single1-eqn} and \eqref{single2-eqn} are functions of a single variable, and the same argument as in the proof of the theorem above yields the second equality in the sequence

\begin{equation}\notag \dev_y(\bd^{(1,0)}f) = \frac{\d}{\d x}(\dev_y f) = \dev_y g_{(1,0)}, \end{equation}

while the first equality follows from the definitions \eqref{def-par-eqn}. Thus, for every $x,y\in \bbr$, we have

\begin{equation}\notag \bd^{(1,0)}f(x,y) = \dev_y(\bd^{(1,0)}f)(x) = \dev_y g_{(1,0)}(x) = g_{(1,0)}(x,y). \end{equation}

A similar argument with the maps $\dev^x$ shows that

\begin{equation}\notag \bd^{(0,1)}f(x,y) = g_{(0,1)}(x,y), \quad \forall x,y\in \bbr. \end{equation}

Therefore, we have

\begin{equation}\notag \bd^{(1,0)} f_n \to \bd^{(1,0)}f \quad \text{and} \quad \bd^{(0,1)} f_n \to \bd^{(0,1)}f \end{equation}

uniformly.

Now, suppose that we have $\bd^\alpha f =g_\alpha$ for all $\alpha \in \bbn^2$ with $|\alpha| =l$, where $l\geq 1$. By replacing $f_n$ with $\bd^\alpha f_n$ and $f$ with $g_\alpha = \bd^\alpha f$ in the above argument, we get that

\begin{equation}\notag \bd^{\alpha +(1,0)}f = g_{\alpha+(1,0)} \quad \text{and} \quad \bd^{\alpha + (0,1)}f = g_{\alpha + (0,1)}. \end{equation}

Thus, we have that $\bd^\alpha f = g_\alpha$ for all $\alpha \in \bbn^2$ with $|\alpha | = l+1$. By induction, we therefore get $\bd^\alpha f = g$ for all $\alpha$, and so

\begin{equation}\notag \bd^\alpha f_n \to \bd^\alpha f \end{equation}

for all $\alpha$. Hence, $f_n \to f$ in the locally convex topology on $C^\infty(\bbr^2;K)$ generated by the seminorms \eqref{par-der-eqn}. Q.E.D.

References
